idioms.definition: In a rural area that is far from towns and cities; in a remote location with few amenities
examples: They live out in the sticks, miles from the nearest shop or petrol station.
I grew up in the sticks, so moving to the city was quite a cultural shock.
The conference centre is lovely, but it really is in the sticks.
idioms.usageTip: Informal and slightly humorous. Can imply isolation or inconvenience, though it is not always used negatively. 'Out in the sticks' is also common.

idioms.definition: Very small or very young — used humorously to describe someone, especially a child, at an earlier time in their life
examples: I've known her since she was knee-high to a grasshopper.
He was knee-high to a grasshopper when his family first moved to this street.
Those twins were knee-high to a grasshopper last time I saw them – now they're teenagers!
idioms.usageTip: Always refers to a past state, never the present. Used humorously to emphasise how young or small someone was at a particular time. Common in informal, nostalgic conversation.

idioms.definition: The best, most modern, or most impressive example of something
examples: This new hotel is the last word in luxury.
The car's interior was the last word in modern design.
Her new apartment is the last word in minimalist living.
idioms.usageTip: Always followed by a noun. Means the ultimate or most refined example of something. Often used in descriptions, advertising, and reviews.

idioms.definition: Including everything; completely; with all parts, possessions, or components included and nothing left out
examples: When they sold the business, they sold everything lock, stock and barrel.
She moved lock, stock and barrel to a new city after getting the job offer.
The company was acquired lock, stock and barrel by a foreign investor.
idioms.usageTip: Comes from the three main parts of a musket (the lock, stock, and barrel). Means the whole thing, without exception. Often used with verbs like sell, buy, move, or transfer.

idioms.definition: A small, visible part of a much larger problem or situation that remains hidden
examples: The complaints we've received so far are just the tip of the iceberg.
The financial irregularities uncovered in the first week were merely the tip of the iceberg.
What we discovered in the initial audit turned out to be only the tip of the iceberg.
idioms.usageTip: Implies that the visible problem is far smaller than the hidden, underlying issue. Very common in journalism, politics, and discussions of large-scale problems.

idioms.definition: Something that seems bad or unfortunate at first but ultimately leads to a good or beneficial outcome
examples: Losing that job was a blessing in disguise – it led me to a much better opportunity.
The delay turned out to be a blessing in disguise; it gave them time to improve the design significantly.
Moving to the countryside seemed like a step back, but it was a blessing in disguise.
idioms.usageTip: Used when a negative experience unexpectedly produces a positive result. One of the most widely used idioms in everyday English. Can also be used attributively: 'a blessing-in-disguise moment'.

fillInExercises.sentence: The retreat was wonderful, but it was well ___ – the nearest town was thirty kilometres away.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'In the sticks' means a remote rural area that is far from towns, cities, and amenities.

fillInExercises.sentence: I last saw my cousin when she was ___ – she must be almost thirty years old now.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Knee-high to a grasshopper' humorously describes someone who was very young or small at a given time in the past.

fillInExercises.sentence: The new spa is ___ relaxation – every detail has been exquisitely thought through.
options: the tip of the iceberg for
fillInExercises.explanation: 'The last word in' means the best or most advanced example of something.

fillInExercises.sentence: When the factory closed, they sold off all the equipment and machinery ___.
options: at the tip of the iceberg
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Lock, stock and barrel' means everything; completely; with nothing left out or behind.

fillInExercises.sentence: The few cases reported to the helpline were just ___ – the real scale of the problem was far greater.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'The tip of the iceberg' means the small visible part of a much larger, hidden problem.

fillInExercises.sentence: Losing the contract seemed disastrous at first, but it turned out to be ___ – it freed us to win a far bigger project.
options: the last word in bad luck
fillInExercises.explanation: 'A blessing in disguise' describes something that initially seems bad or unfortunate but ultimately leads to a positive outcome.

quizExercises.instructions: Match each idiom with its correct meaning.

pairs.meaning: in a remote rural area far from towns and cities
very young or small (said humorously of a child)
the best or most advanced example of something
completely; including everything with nothing left out
the visible part of a much larger hidden problem
something that seems bad but ultimately turns out to be good

quizExercises.sentence: The few reported cases were only ___ – the true extent of the problem became clear much later.
options: the last word in bad news
quizExercises.explanation: 'The tip of the iceberg' refers to the small visible part of a much larger, hidden problem.

quizExercises.sentence: Missing the flight was ___ – we caught a later one and completely avoided the terrible traffic at the other end.
options: the last word in bad luck
quizExercises.explanation: 'A blessing in disguise' means something that seems unfortunate at first but leads to a better outcome.

quizExercises.question: Someone describes their holiday cottage as being miles from the nearest village, with no public transport and unreliable internet. Which idiom best describes where it is?
quizExercises.explanation: 'In the sticks' describes a rural location that is far from towns, cities, and modern amenities.

quizExercises.question: A family sells their house and all their furniture, packs up every item they own, and relocates to another country with absolutely nothing left behind. Which idiom describes this?
quizExercises.explanation: 'Lock, stock and barrel' means completely, with everything included – nothing is left out or behind.
